message.content:
Adversarial verifier, HC-GRC agent/pipeline deep audit. Repo /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc. REFUTE this candidate by reading the real code AND the cited spec. Remember: an intentional Phase-0/1 stub (NotImplementedError + IMPLEMENTED=False) is NOT a defect — set is_stub_by_design=true and confirmed=false for those. Default confirmed=false unless it is a genuine defect in implemented logic, a real research-integrity hole, or a real code-vs-spec contradiction.

CANDIDATE (unit p2-control-topology):
title: NIST framework identifier mismatch causes silent constraint failure
severity: critical
location: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc/src/agents/p2_control_topology/agent.py:109-110
problem: The _tag_nist_cluster method checks for framework names with spaces ('NIST 800-53', 'NIST CSF', 'NIST 800-171') but the actual framework identifiers in the data are kebab-cased ('NIST-800-53', 'NIST-CSF', 'NIST-800-171') per FRAMEWORK_RELATIONSHIPS.md. This mismatch means the NIST cluster tagging will never match actual framework values, causing nodes to be incorrectly tagged as nist_cluster=False when they should be True.
spec_or_invariant: FRAMEWORK_RELATIONSHIPS.md identifies frameworks as 'NIST-800-53', 'NIST-CSF', 'NIST-800-171'; AGENT.md line 77 requires 'NIST 800-53, CSF, 800-171 node attributes are tagged as one authorship cluster'; ADR-0006 requires NIST cluster constraint enforcement at data layer
proposed_fix: Change the framework identifier set to use kebab-case: {'NIST-800-53', 'NIST-CSF', 'NIST-800-171'} to match the canonical identifiers in FRAMEWORK_RELATIONSHIPS.md. Verify that the source control metadata uses these exact identifiers or add case/format normalization.

Verify against actual code + spec (read-only). Cite file:line and the exact spec clause. Be honest about severity.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	"""
2	P2 Agent — Control Space Topology.
3	
4	PURPOSE:
5	Constructs a directed graph from STRM mappings (controls as nodes, mappings
6	as edges) and characterizes the network's mathematical properties. Identifies
7	hub controls, bridge controls, isolated domains, and community structure.
8	
9	PROTECTED AGENT: Prompts may not be modified autonomously by Agent Evolution.
10	Any modification requires Escalation approval (ADR-0015 §77).
11	
12	WORKFLOW POSITION:
13	  Embedding Agent → P2 → P3 (community assignments), P4 (hub map),
14	                          Statistical Analyst, Report Agent
15	
16	EDGE DIRECTIONALITY:
17	  subset (⊂) and superset (⊃): directed
18	  equivalence (=) and intersection (∩): undirected (add edges in both directions)
19	  strength_score: edge weight; imputed at median if missing (document in codebook)
20	
21	COMMUNITY DETECTION:
22	  Must run at least Leiden AND Louvain — results reported for both.
23	  Single-algorithm community structure is not reported as authoritative.
24	
25	NIST CLUSTER CONSTRAINT:
26	  NIST 800-53, CSF, 800-171 node attributes tagged as one authorship cluster.
27	  Community membership spanning all three is NOT evidence of independent convergence.
28	
29	See AGENT.md: agents/03-analysis/p2-control-topology/AGENT.md
30	"""
31	
32	from __future__ import annotations
33	
34	from pathlib import Path
35	from typing import Any
36	
37	from ...state import HCGRCState
38	from ..base import BaseResearchAgent
39	
40	
41	class P2ControlTopologyAgent(BaseResearchAgent):
42	    """
43	    Control space topology agent.
44	
45	    Inputs (from state + filesystem):
46	        - STRM mappings: data/splits/train.parquet + val.parquet (exploratory)
47	        - Control metadata: ara/artifacts/ (node attributes: domain, framework, maturity)
48	
49	    Produces (written to analysis/):
50	        EXP_P2_graph.graphml         — directed + undirected graph per edge type
51	        EXP_P2_topology.parquet      — degree, betweenness centrality, clustering coeff, PageRank
52	        EXP_P2_communities.parquet   — community ID, size, intra/inter-community density
53	        EXP_P2_hubs.md               — top 50 most central controls with annotations
54	        H2.x_results.parquet         — confirmatory results (post-Gate 2)
55	    """
56	
57	    PROTECTED = True
58	    AGENT_ID = "p2-control-topology"
59	
60	    def __init__(
61	        self,
62	        output_dir: Path | str = Path("analysis"),
63	        community_algorithms: list[str] | None = None,
64	        centrality_timeout_seconds: int = 1800,
65	    ) -> None:
66	        self.output_dir = Path(output_dir)
67	        # Must include at least Leiden + Louvain — robustness requires both
68	        self.community_algorithms = community_algorithms or ["leiden", "louvain"]
69	        # Approximate centrality (sampling) if full computation exceeds timeout
70	        self.centrality_timeout_seconds = centrality_timeout_seconds
71	
72	    def run_exploratory(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
73	        """
74	        Build graph, compute topology metrics, detect communities.
75	
76	        Must NOT access test_ids.
77	        All outputs EXP_-prefixed. No community structure claimed as authoritative
78	        until Leiden + Louvain agree within tolerance.
79	        """
80	        raise NotImplementedError(
81	            "P2 exploratory topology analysis not yet implemented. "
82	            "Phase 1 implementation will: (1) load STRM mappings, "
83	            "(2) construct directed/undirected graph per edge type, "
84	            "(3) compute centrality metrics (batch if needed for scale), "
85	            "(4) run Leiden + Louvain community detection, "
86	            "(5) write EXP_P2_graph.graphml, topology.parquet, communities.parquet, hubs.md."
87	        )
88	
89	    def run_confirmatory(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
90	        """
91	        Execute one pre-registered confirmatory test on test split graph.
92	
93	        Gate 2 approval verified by BaseResearchAgent.__call__.
94	        Requires: state["current_hypothesis_id"] == "H2.[n]"
95	        """
96	        raise NotImplementedError(
97	            "P2 confirmatory analysis not yet implemented. "
98	            "Requires: Gate 2 approved, hypothesis_id in state."
99	        )
100	
101	    def _tag_nist_cluster(self, node_attrs: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:
102	        """
103	        Add nist_cluster=True to node attributes for NIST framework nodes.
104	
105	        Must be applied during graph construction so community detection
106	        reports can flag NIST-internal communities.
107	        """
108	        framework = node_attrs.get("framework", "")
109	        node_attrs["nist_cluster"] = framework.upper() in {
110	            "NIST 800-53", "NIST CSF", "NIST 800-171"
111	        }
112	        return node_attrs
113	
114	    def _impute_missing_strength(
115	        self,
116	        strength_score: float | None,
117	        median_strength: float,
118	    ) -> tuple[float, bool]:
119	        """
120	        Impute missing edge weight at median. Returns (value, was_imputed).
121	
122	        Imputation must be logged in codebook — all imputed edges labeled
123	        strength_imputed=True in the GraphML output.
124	        """
125	        if strength_score is None:
126	            return median_strength, True
127	        return strength_score, False
128

content.content:
1	# Framework Relationships Constitution — How GRC Frameworks Relate to Each Other and to the SCF
2	
3	This document encodes the structural relationships between frameworks in the hc-grc program and the SCF's methodology for mapping them. It is the reference for how cross-framework analysis is conducted and what claims it can and cannot support. The program is open-ended — the current study set is the first wave, not a ceiling.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. The Current Study Set
8	
9	The program begins with five frameworks. Expansion to additional frameworks is expected. Each new framework added must be evaluated against the independence constraints in section 2, the licensing requirements in section 8, and the crosswalk availability in section 9 before inclusion.
10	
11	| Framework | Code | Publisher | License | Role in hc-grc |
12	|-----------|------|-----------|---------|----------------|
13	| Secure Controls Framework | SCF | SCF Council / ComplianceForge | Creative Commons | Corpus + crosswalk substrate |
14	| NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 | NIST-800-53 | NIST | Public domain (OSCAL) | Native corpus |
15	| NIST CSF 2.0 | NIST-CSF | NIST | Public domain | Native corpus |
16	| NIST SP 800-171 Rev 3 | NIST-800-171 | NIST | Public domain (OSCAL) | Native corpus |
17	| CIS Controls v8 | CIS-v8 | Center for Internet Security | Open terms | Native corpus |
18	
19	---
20	
21	## 2. The NIST Cluster — The Critical Independence Constraint
22	
23	Three frameworks in the current study set share a common author: NIST. This is not a minor observation. It is a structural constraint on what cross-framework findings can claim. Any future NIST-derived framework added to the program (e.g., NIST 800-171A, NIST AI RMF) extends this cluster and does not add an independent evidence source.
24	
25	**NIST SP 800-53** is the comprehensive security and privacy controls catalog for federal information systems. It is the most expansive NIST framework — the source document from which many others derive.
26	
27	**NIST CSF 2.0** is the Cybersecurity Framework — a risk-based framework organized around five functions (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover) with a sixth added in version 2.0 (Govern). It is designed for broad adoption across sectors, abstracting NIST 800-53 into higher-level outcomes.
28	
29	**NIST SP 800-171** governs protection of Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) in non-federal systems. It is a subset of 800-53, scoped for defense contractors and federal supply chain.
30	
31	These three frameworks share:
32	- The same authoring organization (NIST)
33	- Overlapping control populations (800-171 is explicitly derived from 800-53)
34	- Common terminology, taxonomic conventions, and risk framing
35	- The same OSCAL format and schema
36	
37	**The constraint**: Agreement among NIST 800-53, NIST CSF, and NIST 800-171 on any structural finding does not constitute independent replication. It constitutes agreement within a single editorial lineage. The "NIST cluster" (L2 constraint in hc-grc terminology) means:
38	
39	- A finding that holds in all three NIST frameworks but not in any non-NIST framework is a **NIST-cluster property**, not an intrinsic property of control space
40	- A finding that holds across the NIST cluster AND at least one non-NIST framework (SCF, CIS, or any future addition) is a candidate for "intrinsic" classification — subject to further adversarial review
41	- A finding that holds across all frameworks under study, spanning multiple independent authoring organizations, is the program's strongest evidence class
42	
43	---
44	
45	## 3. The SCF as Crosswalk Substrate
46	
47	The SCF is unique among frameworks in the hc-grc program: it was explicitly designed to map to all others via STRM. This gives it a dual role:
48	
49	**As a research subject**: The SCF's own structural properties — domain organization, control density, risk coverage, network topology — are characterized as a corpus alongside the other four frameworks.
50	
51	**As the alignment layer**: Because the SCF maps to NIST 800-53, NIST CSF, NIST 800-171, and CIS Controls (among 200+ others), it provides the common substrate for cross-framework comparison in Tier 2. The crosswalk built from SCF's STRM mappings is what allows the program to ask: does finding X in 800-53 correspond to finding Y in CIS Controls, and what does the SCF's control space look like when both are projected onto it?
52	
53	**The circularity constraint (L1)**: SCF crosswalk evidence cannot establish "structural similarity" between the SCF and any other framework. That would be circular — the SCF defined the crosswalk, so agreement between SCF and any framework's STRM mapping is not independent evidence. It is a tautology. Any cross-framework claim that traces primarily to SCF-to-SCF crosswalk agreement is invalid.
54	
55	---
56	
57	## 4. The STRM Relationship Types — What They Mean for Analysis
58	
59	The SCF's Set Theory Relationship Mapping uses five relationship types from NIST IR 8477:
60	
61	**⊂ Subset Of**: The SCF control is contained within the external requirement. The external requirement demands more than or equal to what the SCF control specifies. An organization implementing the SCF control satisfies part of the requirement but may not satisfy all of it.
62	
63	**∩ Intersects**: Partial overlap. The SCF control and the external requirement share some semantic content but neither contains the other. Implementing the SCF control addresses some but not all of the requirement, and vice versa.
64	
65	**= Equal To**: The SCF control fully satisfies the external requirement and the requirement fully satisfies the SCF control. They are semantically equivalent.
66	
67	**⊃ Superset Of**: The SCF control exceeds the external requirement. Implementing the SCF control satisfies the requirement and provides additional coverage.
68	
69	**∅ No Relation**: No meaningful overlap between the SCF control and the external requirement.
70	
71	**What these mean empirically**: STRM mappings are expert judgments, not measurements. They represent the SCF Council's assessment of semantic overlap. The hc-grc program tests whether these claimed relationships hold in the actual control text — whether controls the STRM marks as "= Equal To" are actually semantically equivalent when the text is embedded and analyzed, or whether "⊃ Superset Of" claims overstate the SCF's coverage relative to the mapped framework.
72	
73	---
74	
75	## 5. Framework Scope Differences — What Each Is For
76	
77	Understanding what each framework was designed to cover is prerequisite to interpreting structural differences.
78	
79	**SCF**: Comprehensive metaframework. Designed to cover every control domain relevant to cybersecurity, privacy, and resilience for organizations of any size or sector. No specific regulatory mandate — designed for voluntary adoption as a compliance consolidation tool.
80	
81	**NIST 800-53**: Comprehensive security and privacy controls for federal information systems and organizations. Required for federal agencies (FISMA). The most granular and extensive NIST framework — 20 control families, 1,000+ controls including enhancements. Includes privacy controls integrated with security controls since Rev 5.
82	
83	**NIST CSF 2.0**: Outcome-based risk framework for broad adoption. Organized by function (Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover) rather than control domain. Maps to 800-53 but at a higher level of abstraction. Not prescriptive — organizations choose their implementation approach.
84	
85	**NIST 800-171**: Subset of 800-53, scoped for CUI protection in non-federal systems. 110 basic security requirements across 14 families. Required for defense contractors (DFARS clause 252.204-7012) and increasingly for broader federal supply chain participation (CMMC).
86	
87	**CIS Controls v8**: 18 controls organized into three implementation groups (IG1/IG2/IG3) scaled by organizational size and risk profile. Prescriptive and prioritized — designed for practical implementation, not comprehensive coverage. Based on observed attack patterns (not regulatory requirements).
88	
89	**Structural implications**:
90	- 800-53 and SCF are comprehensive; CSF and CIS Controls are selective
91	- 800-171 is a derivative subset — it does not introduce novel control concepts, only scopes and combines elements from 800-53
92	- CIS Controls are attack-surface-driven; the others are more domain-completeness-driven
93	- These design differences should produce measurable structural differences in clustering, network topology, and coverage distribution — and those differences are findings, not artifacts
94	
95	---
96	
97	## 6. What Cross-Framework Replication Means
98	
99	The hc-grc program's strongest evidence is a structural finding that replicates across the frameworks under study. This requires understanding what "replication" means across frameworks with different scope, design intent, and authorship. As the program expands, additional frameworks increase the available replication surface — but only those from independent authoring organizations add independent evidence.
100	
101	**Intrinsic property**: A structural finding that holds across all frameworks under study — including across the NIST cluster AND at least one non-NIST framework — is a candidate for "intrinsic" classification. It suggests the finding is a property of control space itself, not an artifact of any particular framework's design philosophy. Intrinsic findings are the most strategically significant — they describe the underlying landscape that every framework is trying to map. The more independent authoring organizations represented in the replication set, the stronger the intrinsic claim.
102	
103	**Framework-specific property**: A finding that appears in some frameworks but not others. This is evidence about those frameworks' editorial choices — what they chose to emphasize, scope out, or organize differently. Framework-specific findings are significant for framework selection decisions: if your risk profile is concentrated in an area one framework handles differently than others, that is material information.
104	
105	**Crosswalk-dependent property**: A finding that appears only when frameworks are aligned via the SCF crosswalk. This finding is conditional on the quality of the crosswalk alignment — a noisy or incorrect crosswalk mapping could produce spurious agreements or hide genuine differences. Crosswalk-dependent findings must always be reported with their alignment quality caveat.
106	
107	**The L2 independence constraint, restated**: A finding that holds only within the NIST cluster (800-53, CSF, 800-171) is not independent replication. Three findings that share authorship, terminology, and derivation history are one evidence source, not three.
108	
109	---
110	
111	## 7. The SCF's Relationship to Compliance Frameworks
112	
113	The SCF maps to the following categories of external frameworks (partial list from 200+):
114	
115	**US Federal**: FISMA, FedRAMP, CMMC, DFARS, SOX (IT), GLBA, HIPAA, FDA 21 CFR Part 11, NERC CIP, TSA security directives
116	
117	**International**: GDPR, NIS2, DORA, ISO 27001/27002, ISO 31000, ISO 31010
118	
119	**Industry**: PCI DSS, HITRUST CSF, SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria, COBIT, CSA CCM, OWASP
120	
121	**State/Regional**: CCPA, NY DFS Cybersecurity Regulation, Texas DIR
122	
123	For each mapping, STRM specifies the relationship type (⊂, ∩, =, ⊃, ∅) and a strength score. The density and type distribution of these mappings across SCF domains is empirically measurable — and reveals which SCF domains are structurally central (mapped to by many external frameworks) versus peripheral (few external framework mappings).
124	
125	This is a direct measure of **regulatory coverage concentration** — which control domains regulators and industry bodies have converged on versus which they have largely ignored.
126	
127	---
128	
129	## 8. Framework Licensing and Its Significance
130	
131	All frameworks in the hc-grc program's first wave are open-licensed. This is deliberate — it means:
132	
133	- Source text is freely available for ingestion without license negotiation (T2 trivial)
134	- The corpus can be published as part of a reproducibility package
135	- Findings are based on the full authoritative text, not summaries or abstracts
136	
137	**SCF**: Creative Commons. The SCF Council explicitly permits analysis and derivative works under CC terms.
138	
139	**NIST frameworks**: Public domain (US government works). OSCAL format is published on GitHub with no restrictions.
140	
141	**CIS Controls v8**: Available under CIS open terms for non-commercial analysis.
142	
143	A future expansion to paywalled frameworks (ISO 27001, PCI DSS full text) requires T2 sign-off due to licensing implications — this is the human gate the program preserves for that decision.
144	
145	---
146	
147	## 9. The Crosswalk's Role in Tier 2 Analysis
148	
149	Tier 2 of the hc-grc program (cross-framework synthesis) depends on the SCF crosswalk as its alignment substrate. The mechanics:
150	
151	1. Each framework's controls are mapped to SCF controls via STRM relationships
152	2. Controls that map to the same SCF control are placed in "alignment" for comparison
153	3. Structural findings from each framework's Tier 1 study are compared at the SCF-aligned level
154	4. Findings that hold across aligned controls from multiple frameworks are candidates for cross-framework claims
155	
156	**What this analysis can and cannot claim**:
157	- It CAN identify which structural properties survive framework alignment — i.e., are visible from multiple frameworks' perspectives on the same underlying control space
158	- It CANNOT claim that frameworks with high SCF alignment scores are "better" or "more comprehensive" — that is a normative judgment, not a structural finding
159	- It CAN identify which risk categories (from the 39) are well-covered by multiple frameworks and which are addressed by only one or none
160	- It CANNOT resolve disagreements between frameworks' STRM mappings by fiat — where STRM claims equivalence but textual analysis finds divergence, both are reported
161	
162	The crosswalk is a measurement instrument, not a ground truth. Its quality is itself a finding, and every cross-framework claim must carry its alignment quality as a caveat.
163

1	---
2	name: p2-control-topology
3	description: Analyzes the structural topology of the SCF control space as a graph — mapping control relationships as edges, detecting clusters, identifying hub controls, measuring domain connectivity, and characterizing the mathematical properties of the control network. Produces the first formal graph-theoretic characterization of a security metaframework at this scale.
4	version: 1.0.0
5	team: 03-analysis
6	status: primary
7	trigger: always
8	author: HC-GRC
9	tags: [P2, Graph Analysis, Network Topology, Control Space, Clustering, Community Detection]
10	skills: [ray-data]
11	tools: [mcp-qdrant, mcp-mlflow, mcp-lab-notebook]
12	---
13	
14	# P2 Agent — Control Space Topology
15	
16	## Purpose
17	
18	1,400 controls connected by 280,000 mappings form a graph. That graph has properties — hubs, bridges, isolated clusters, densely connected communities — that are invisible when you read controls one at a time. P2 makes those properties visible and measurable. Which controls are most central to the entire framework? Which domains are structurally isolated from the rest? Where are the bridges between apparently unrelated compliance regimes? The topology findings directly inform P3 (convergence), P4 (risk blindspots), and P5 (governance clustering).
19	
20	## Position in Workflow
21	
22	Runs in parallel with P1 after Embedding Agent and Gate 2. Topology analysis is largely exploratory; confirmatory hypotheses focus on specific structural predictions.
23	
24	```
25	[Embedding Agent: Qdrant collections]
26	[Data Steward: splits]
27	        ↓
28	[P2 Agent]
29	  ├── Graph construction from STRM mappings
30	  ├── EXP_ topology metrics (degree, centrality, clustering coefficient)
31	  ├── Community detection (Leiden, Louvain)
32	  └── H2.x confirmatory tests
33	        ↓
34	[P3 Agent] and [P4 Agent] and [Statistical Analyst]
35	```
36	
37	## Inputs
38	
39	| Input | Source | Format | Schema / Notes |
40	|-------|--------|--------|----------------|
41	| STRM mappings | data/splits/ | Parquet | Edges: source control, target control, relationship type, strength |
42	| Control metadata | ara/artifacts/ | JSON | Node attributes: domain, framework, maturity level, MCR/DSR classification |
43	| Registered hypotheses | docs/protocol/03_statistical_analysis_plan.md | JSON | H2.x hypotheses with test specifications |
44	
45	## Outputs / Artifacts
46	
47	| Artifact | Destination | Format | Schema / Notes |
48	|----------|-------------|--------|----------------|
49	| Control graph | analysis/01-exploratory/EXP_P2_graph.graphml | GraphML | Nodes: controls; edges: STRM mappings with type + strength attributes |
50	| Topology metrics | analysis/01-exploratory/EXP_P2_topology.parquet | Parquet | Degree, betweenness centrality, clustering coefficient, PageRank per control |
51	| Community assignments | analysis/01-exploratory/EXP_P2_communities.parquet | Parquet | Community ID, size, intra/inter-community edge density |
52	| Hub control register | analysis/01-exploratory/EXP_P2_hubs.md | Markdown | Top 50 most central controls with domain and framework annotations |
53	| Confirmatory results | analysis/02-confirmatory/H2.x_results.parquet | Parquet | Per-hypothesis test results |
54	
55	## Tools & MCP Servers
56	
57	| Tool | Purpose | MCP Server | Notes |
58	|------|---------|-----------|-------|
59	| Qdrant | Query control metadata for node attributes | mcp-qdrant | Metadata filtering by domain, framework, maturity |
60	| MLflow | Track graph construction parameters and metrics | mcp-mlflow | Graph density, N nodes, N edges per run |
61	| Lab notebook | Record topology findings and anomalies | mcp-lab-notebook | Append-only |
62	
63	## Skills Used
64	
65	- **Ray Data** — Parallel graph construction and metric computation across 280,000+ edges. Centrality computations parallelized via Ray for performance on dense graphs.
66	
67	## Handoffs
68	
69	**Receives from**: Data Steward (splits), Embedding Agent (Qdrant metadata), ARA artifacts (node attributes)
70	**Passes to**: P3 Agent (community assignments for cross-framework analysis), P4 Agent (hub map for blindspot detection), Statistical Analyst (H2.x test inputs), Report Agent (topology visualizations)
71	**Human gate**: Gate 3 — human reviews community structure and hub control register before confirmatory analysis
72	
73	## Behavioral Constraints
74	- **Protected research agent:** This agent's prompts may not be modified autonomously by Agent Evolution. Any prompt modification requires Escalation approval before taking effect. Modifying this agent mid-tier against SCF corpus data constitutes a research design change that bypasses Gate 2 (per ADR-0015, #77).
75	
76	- Graph construction uses STRM relationship types as directed edge types — subset (⊂) and superset (⊃) edges are directed; equivalence (=) and intersection (∩) are undirected.
77	- NIST Cluster Independence Constraint applies — NIST 800-53, CSF, 800-171 node attributes are tagged as one authorship cluster in community analysis.
78	- Strength scores used as edge weights; missing strength scores imputed at median (documented in codebook).
79	- All exploratory graph metrics labeled EXP_ — no confirmatory claims from topology exploration.
80	
81	## Failure Modes & Recovery
82	
83	| Failure | Detection | Recovery |
84	|---------|-----------|----------|
85	| Graph too sparse for community detection | Edge density < 0.001 | Log finding; report as structural characteristic; do not force community structure |
86	| Centrality computation timeout on full graph | > 30min runtime | Use approximate centrality (sampling); log approximation parameters |
87	| Disconnected graph components | N components > 1 | Analyze largest component; document isolated controls separately |
88	
89	## Evaluation Criteria
90	
91	- [ ] Graph constructed from 100% of STRM mappings in analysis split
92	- [ ] Community detection run with at least 2 algorithms (Leiden + Louvain) for robustness
93	- [ ] Hub controls validated against domain expert expectations (QA Agent spot check)
94	- [ ] NIST cluster constraint documented in graph metadata
95	- [ ] All confirmatory scripts have SAP headers validated by Code Review Agent
96	
97	## Notes
98	
99	The graph is both a research artifact and an analytical substrate. P3, P4, and P5 all query the P2 graph structure. The GraphML export is versioned in DVC and referenced by downstream agents — any re-run of P2 that changes graph structure requires all downstream modules to re-run as well.
100

content.content:
1	"""
2	P1 Agent — SCF NLP Characterization (STRM vs. Computational Similarity).
3	
4	PURPOSE:
5	Applies NLP similarity methods to every STRM mapping and compares computational
6	findings to STRM expert labels. The gap between the two is the primary finding.
7	
8	PROTECTED AGENT: Prompts may not be modified autonomously by Agent Evolution.
9	Any modification requires Escalation approval (ADR-0015 §77).
10	
11	WORKFLOW POSITION:
12	  Embedding Agent (FAISS + Qdrant) → P1 → Statistical Analyst, QA Agent
13	
14	PHASE DISCIPLINE:
15	  Exploratory: train + val splits. All outputs prefixed EXP_. No p-value decisions.
16	  Confirmatory: test split only, after Gate 2. Every script has SAP header.
17	
18	NIST CLUSTER CONSTRAINT:
19	  NIST 800-53, CSF, and 800-171 share authorship — treated as ONE evidence source.
20	  Cross-framework findings citing all three as independent validation are invalid.
21	
22	NLP METHOD TIERS (all must run in exploratory phase — no cherry-picking):
23	  1. Lexical: TF-IDF cosine, BM25
24	  2. Embedding: cosine similarity over dense embeddings (5 model candidates)
25	  3. Topic: LDA/BERTopic coherence correlation
26	  4. Entailment: NLI model on (source, target) pairs
27	  5. Hybrid: weighted ensemble of tiers 1-4
28	
29	See AGENT.md: agents/03-analysis/p1-strm-nlp/AGENT.md
30	"""
31	
32	from __future__ import annotations
33	
34	from pathlib import Path
35	from typing import Any
36	
37	from ...state import HCGRCState
38	from ..base import BaseResearchAgent
39	
40	
41	class P1StrmNlpAgent(BaseResearchAgent):
42	    """
43	    SCF NLP Characterization agent.
44	
45	    Inputs (from state):
46	        - train_ids / val_ids / test_ids: split control ID lists
47	        - data_split_seed: for reproducibility logging
48	        - run_id: for MLflow experiment tagging and PROV-DM
49	
50	    Produces (written to analysis/):
51	        EXP_P1_similarity.parquet   — all-pairs similarity across NLP method tiers
52	        EXP_P1_classification.parquet — predicted relationship types vs. STRM labels
53	        EXP_P1_disagreements.md     — high-disagreement mappings for Gate 3 review
54	        H1.x_results.parquet       — confirmatory per-hypothesis results (post-Gate 2)
55	    """
56	
57	    PROTECTED = True
58	    AGENT_ID = "p1-strm-nlp"
59	
60	    def __init__(
61	        self,
62	        output_dir: Path | str = Path("analysis"),
63	        embedding_model_id: str | None = None,
64	        nist_cluster_ids: set[str] | None = None,
65	    ) -> None:
66	        self.output_dir = Path(output_dir)
67	        self.embedding_model_id = embedding_model_id
68	        # Controls from NIST 800-53, CSF, 800-171 — one authorship cluster
69	        self.nist_cluster_ids: set[str] = nist_cluster_ids or set()
70	
71	    # ── Exploratory phase ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
72	
73	    def run_exploratory(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
74	        """
75	        Run all 5 NLP method tiers on train + val splits.
76	
77	        MUST NOT access test_ids.
78	        All outputs written to analysis/01-exploratory/ with EXP_ prefix.
79	        No p-value decision language in any output.
80	        """
81	        raise NotImplementedError(
82	            "P1 exploratory analysis not yet implemented. "
83	            "Phase 1 implementation will: (1) load STRM mappings from "
84	            "data/splits/train.parquet + val.parquet, (2) run all 5 NLP tiers, "
85	            "(3) write EXP_P1_similarity.parquet and EXP_P1_classification.parquet, "
86	            "(4) generate EXP_P1_disagreements.md for Gate 3."
87	        )
88	
89	    # ── Confirmatory phase ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
90	
91	    def run_confirmatory(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
92	        """
93	        Execute one pre-registered confirmatory test on the test split.
94	
95	        Gate 2 approval verified by BaseResearchAgent.__call__ before this runs.
96	        Requires: state["current_hypothesis_id"] == "H1.[n]"
97	        Writes:   analysis/02-confirmatory/H1.[n]_results.parquet
98	
99	        SAP header contract (enforced by Code Review Agent):
100	            # SAP: hypothesis_id=H1.[n]
101	            # test=[named test], effect_size_threshold=[value]
102	            # decision_rule=[criterion]
103	        """
104	        raise NotImplementedError(
105	            "P1 confirmatory analysis not yet implemented. "
106	            "Requires: Gate 2 approved, hypothesis_id in state, "
107	            "test split accessible via DataSteward."
108	        )
109	
110	    # ── NIST cluster constraint enforcement ───────────────────────────────────
111	
112	    def _is_nist_cluster(self, framework: str) -> bool:
113	        """
114	        Return True if framework is in the NIST authorship cluster.
115	
116	        NIST 800-53, CSF, and 800-171 share authorship. Citing agreement
117	        between all three as independent validation is a SAP violation.
118	        """
119	        return framework.upper() in {"NIST 800-53", "NIST CSF", "NIST 800-171"}
120	
121	    def _validate_cross_framework_claim(
122	        self,
123	        framework_a: str,
124	        framework_b: str,
125	    ) -> None:
126	        """
127	        Raise ValueError if claim crosses NIST cluster without disclosing constraint.
128	
129	        Call before writing any cross-framework similarity finding.
130	        """
131	        if self._is_nist_cluster(framework_a) and self._is_nist_cluster(framework_b):
132	            raise ValueError(
133	                f"Cross-framework finding between {framework_a!r} and {framework_b!r} "
134	                "violates NIST Cluster Independence Constraint. These frameworks share "
135	                "authorship and cannot be cited as independent validation. "
136	                "Tag finding as NIST_CLUSTER_INTERNAL and exclude from convergence claims."
137	            )
138

content.text: Now let me check the P3 agent which also has a similar check:

content.content:
1	"""
2	P3 Agent — Regulatory Convergence Atlas.
3	
4	PURPOSE:
5	Measures pairwise cross-framework semantic and structural convergence across
6	all 33 SCF domains. Distinguishes GENUINE convergence (semantic alignment)
7	from TERMINOLOGICAL convergence (similar words, different meaning). Produces
8	the Convergence Atlas — a quantified map of where frameworks actually agree.
9	
10	PROTECTED AGENT: Prompts may not be modified autonomously by Agent Evolution.
11	Any modification requires Escalation approval (ADR-0015 §77).
12	
13	WORKFLOW POSITION:
14	  P2 (community assignments) → P3 → P4 (convergence gaps inform blindspot detection),
15	                                     Statistical Analyst, Report Agent
16	
17	CONVERGENCE TYPES (must classify every finding):
18	  - GENUINE:          semantic alignment — embeddings agree, STRM labels confirm
19	  - TERMINOLOGICAL:   similar words, different semantic content — must never be
20	                      reported as genuine convergence
21	  - STRUCTURAL:       same position in graph topology without semantic agreement
22	
23	NIST CLUSTER CONSTRAINT (MANDATORY):
24	  NIST 800-53, CSF, 800-171 are ONE authorship cluster.
25	  Convergence between them is NIST_CLUSTER_INTERNAL — excluded from cross-framework
26	  convergence claims. This constraint applies to every framework pair computation.
27	
28	CLASSIFIER CONFIDENCE THRESHOLD:
29	  Convergence type classification (LLM via Instructor/Pydantic) requires
30	  confidence ≥ 0.70. Below threshold → human review flag; do not auto-classify.
31	
32	See AGENT.md: agents/03-analysis/p3-regulatory-convergence/AGENT.md
33	"""
34	
35	from __future__ import annotations
36	
37	from pathlib import Path
38	from typing import Any, Literal
39	
40	from ...state import HCGRCState
41	from ..base import BaseResearchAgent
42	
43	# Convergence classification types
44	ConvergenceType = Literal["genuine", "terminological", "structural", "nist_cluster_internal"]
45	
46	
47	class P3RegulatoryConvergenceAgent(BaseResearchAgent):
48	    """
49	    Regulatory convergence atlas agent.
50	
51	    Inputs (from state + filesystem):
52	        - P2 community assignments: analysis/01-exploratory/EXP_P2_communities.parquet
53	        - Qdrant: framework-tagged control embeddings
54	        - STRM equivalence mappings: data/splits/ (relationship_type == "equal")
55	
56	    Produces:
57	        EXP_P3_convergence.parquet  — framework × framework × domain convergence scores
58	        EXP_P3_atlas.json           — structured atlas: genuine, terminological, divergence
59	        EXP_P3_heatmap.parquet      — input to Visualization Agent
60	        H3.x_results.parquet        — confirmatory results (post-Gate 2)
61	    """
62	
63	    PROTECTED = True
64	    AGENT_ID = "p3-regulatory-convergence"
65	
66	    # Classifier confidence threshold — below this, flag for human review
67	    CLASSIFIER_CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD = 0.70
68	
69	    # False convergence prevalence threshold — above this, Gate 3 discussion item
70	    FALSE_CONVERGENCE_GATE3_THRESHOLD = 0.05  # 5%
71	
72	    def __init__(
73	        self,
74	        output_dir: Path | str = Path("analysis"),
75	        classifier_model: str | None = None,
76	    ) -> None:
77	        self.output_dir = Path(output_dir)
78	        # LLM used for convergence type classification (Instructor/Pydantic)
79	        # Must be local — no SaaS inference
80	        self.classifier_model = classifier_model
81	
82	    def run_exploratory(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
83	        """
84	        Compute pairwise framework convergence across all 33 domains.
85	
86	        Must NOT access test_ids.
87	        All outputs EXP_-prefixed.
88	        NIST cluster constraint applied to every framework pair.
89	        Terminological convergence classified and never reported as genuine.
90	        """
91	        raise NotImplementedError(
92	            "P3 exploratory convergence analysis not yet implemented. "
93	            "Phase 1 implementation will: (1) load P2 community assignments, "
94	            "(2) compute pairwise cosine similarity between framework-specific "
95	            "control embeddings per domain, (3) classify convergence type via "
96	            "Instructor+Pydantic (confidence >= 0.70), (4) apply NIST cluster "
97	            "constraint to all framework pairs, (5) write convergence.parquet, "
98	            "atlas.json, heatmap.parquet."
99	        )
100	
101	    def run_confirmatory(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
102	        """
103	        Execute one pre-registered confirmatory convergence test on test split.
104	
105	        Gate 2 approval verified by BaseResearchAgent.__call__.
106	        Requires: state["current_hypothesis_id"] == "H3.[n]"
107	        """
108	        raise NotImplementedError(
109	            "P3 confirmatory analysis not yet implemented. "
110	            "Requires: Gate 2 approved, hypothesis_id in state."
111	        )
112	
113	    def _is_nist_cluster_pair(self, fw_a: str, fw_b: str) -> bool:
114	        """
115	        Return True if both frameworks are in the NIST authorship cluster.
116	
117	        Pairs that return True must be tagged NIST_CLUSTER_INTERNAL and
118	        excluded from cross-framework convergence claims.
119	        """
120	        nist = {"NIST 800-53", "NIST CSF", "NIST 800-171"}
121	        return fw_a.upper() in nist and fw_b.upper() in nist
122	
123	    def _classify_convergence(
124	        self,
125	        similarity_score: float,
126	        strm_relationship: str,
127	        linguistic_overlap: float,
128	    ) -> tuple[ConvergenceType, float]:
129	        """
130	        Classify convergence type and return (type, confidence).
131	
132	        In Phase 1 implementation: calls local LLM via Instructor with a
133	        typed Pydantic ConvergenceClassification output schema.
134	
135	        If confidence < CLASSIFIER_CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD, returns
136	        ("terminological", confidence) and flags for human review —
137	        do NOT auto-classify uncertain cases.
138	        """
139	        raise NotImplementedError(
140	            "Convergence classification requires Instructor + local LLM. "
141	            "Not implemented until Phase 1 LLM infrastructure is available."
142	        )
143

content.text: Now let me look at the actual specification to understand what format the framework identifiers should be in. Let me check the FRAMEWORK_RELATIONSHIPS.md again more carefully:

content.content:
| Framework | Code | Publisher | License | Role in hc-grc |
| NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 | NIST-800-53 | NIST | Public domain (OSCAL) | Native corpus |
| NIST CSF 2.0 | NIST-CSF | NIST | Public domain | Native corpus |
| NIST SP 800-171 Rev 3 | NIST-800-171 | NIST | Public domain (OSCAL) | Native corpus |
## 2. The NIST Cluster — The Critical Independence Constraint
Three frameworks in the current study set share a common author: NIST. This is not a minor observation. It is a structural constraint on what cross-framework findings can claim. Any future NIST-derived framework added to the program (e.g., NIST 800-171A, NIST AI RMF) extends this cluster and does not add an independent evidence source.
**NIST SP 800-53** is the comprehensive security and privacy controls catalog for federal information systems. It is the most expansive NIST framework — the source document from which many others derive.
**NIST CSF 2.0** is the Cybersecurity Framework — a risk-based framework organized around five functions (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover) with a sixth added in version 2.0 (Govern). It is designed for broad adoption across sectors, abstracting NIST 800-53 into higher-level outcomes.
**NIST SP 800-171** governs protection of Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) in non-federal systems. It is a subset of 800-53, scoped for defense contractors and federal supply chain.
- The same authoring organization (NIST)
**The constraint**: Agreement among NIST 800-53, NIST CSF, and NIST 800-171 on any structural finding does not constitute independent replication. It constitutes agreement within a single editorial lineage. The "NIST cluster" (L2 constraint in hc-grc terminology) means:
- A finding that holds in all three NIST frameworks but not in any non-NIST framework is a **NIST-cluster property**, not an intrinsic property of control space
- A finding that holds across the NIST cluster AND at least one non-NIST framework (SCF, CIS, or any future addition) is a candidate for "intrinsic" classification — subject to further adversarial review
**As the alignment layer**: Because the SCF maps to NIST 800-53, NIST CSF, NIST 800-171, and CIS Controls (among 200+ others), it provides the common substrate for cross-framework comparison in Tier 2. The crosswalk built from SCF's STRM mappings is what allows the program to ask: does finding X in 800-53 correspond to finding Y in CIS Controls, and what does the SCF's control space look like when both are projected onto it?
The SCF's Set Theory Relationship Mapping uses five relationship types from NIST IR 8477:
**NIST 800-53**: Comprehensive security and privacy controls for federal information systems and organizations. Required for federal agencies (FISMA). The most granular and extensive NIST framework — 20 control families, 1,000+ controls including enhancements. Includes privacy controls integrated with security controls since Rev 5.
**NIST CSF 2.0**: Outcome-based risk framework for broad adoption. Organized by function (Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover) rather than control domain. Maps to 800-53 but at a higher level of abstraction. Not prescriptive — organizations choose their implementation approach.
**NIST 800-171**: Subset of 800-53, scoped for CUI protection in non-federal systems. 110 basic security requirements across 14 families. Required for defense contractors (DFARS clause 252.204-7012) and increasingly for broader federal supply chain participation (CMMC).
- 800-53 and SCF are comprehensive; CSF and CIS Controls are selective
**Intrinsic property**: A structural finding that holds across all frameworks under study — including across the NIST cluster AND at least one non-NIST framework — is a candidate for "intrinsic" classification. It suggests the finding is a property of control space itself, not an artifact of any particular framework's design philosophy. Intrinsic findings are the most strategically significant — they describe the underlying landscape that every framework is trying to map. The more independent authoring organizations represented in the replication set, the stronger the intrinsic claim.

--
## Handoffs

**Receives from**: Data Steward (splits), Embedding Agent (Qdrant metadata), ARA artifacts (node attributes)
**Passes to**: P3 Agent (community assignments for cross-framework analysis), P4 Agent (hub map for blindspot detection), Statistical Analyst (H2.x test inputs), Report Agent (topology visualizations)
**Human gate**: Gate 3 — human reviews community structure and hub control register before confirmatory analysis
--

- Graph construction uses STRM relationship types as directed edge types — subset (⊂) and superset (⊃) edges are directed; equivalence (=) and intersection (∩) are undirected.
- NIST Cluster Independence Constraint applies — NIST 800-53, CSF, 800-171 node attributes are tagged as one authorship cluster in community analysis.
- Strength scores used as edge weights; missing strength scores imputed at median (documented in codebook).
- All exploratory graph metrics labeled EXP_ — no confirmatory claims from topology exploration.

1	"""
2	HC-GRC agent base types and contracts.
3	
4	All Phase 1 research agents inherit from BaseResearchAgent or implement the
5	same __call__ signature. The contract is:
6	
7	    agent(state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]
8	
9	The return dict is a LangGraph partial state update — merged into HCGRCState
10	by the graph runtime. Agents must never mutate state in place.
11	
12	PROTECTED agents (P1–P5, statistical-analyst, hypothesis-formalizer) have
13	PROTECTED = True. Agent Evolution may not modify their prompts autonomously.
14	Any modification requires Escalation approval (ADR-0015 §77).
15	
16	Phase guard:
17	  - Exploratory phase: access train_ids + val_ids only
18	  - Confirmatory phase: access test_ids — only after Gate 2 approval
19	  - Agents check state["gate_status"]["gate_2"].decision == "approved" before
20	    touching test split. Violation is a SAP violation.
21	
22	EXP_ prefix convention:
23	  - All exploratory artifacts use EXP_ filename prefix
24	  - No p-value decision language in EXP_ outputs
25	  - Confirmatory scripts carry a SAP header with hypothesis ID and decision rule
26	"""
27	
28	from __future__ import annotations
29	
30	from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
31	from typing import Any, Literal
32	
33	from ..state import HCGRCState
34	
35	# ── Shared input types ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
36	
37	class STRMMapping:
38	    """
39	    A single STRM mapping between two SCF controls.
40	
41	    In Phase 0 (synthetic): populated with placeholder values.
42	    In Phase 1+: populated from data/splits/*.parquet.
43	    """
44	    __slots__ = (
45	        "source_control_id", "target_control_id",
46	        "relationship_type", "strength_score",
47	        "source_domain", "target_domain",
48	        "source_framework", "target_framework",
49	    )
50	
51	    def __init__(
52	        self,
53	        source_control_id: str,
54	        target_control_id: str,
55	        relationship_type: Literal["subset", "superset", "intersection", "equal", "no_relation"],
56	        strength_score: float | None = None,
57	        source_domain: str | None = None,
58	        target_domain: str | None = None,
59	        source_framework: str | None = None,
60	        target_framework: str | None = None,
61	    ) -> None:
62	        self.source_control_id = source_control_id
63	        self.target_control_id = target_control_id
64	        self.relationship_type = relationship_type
65	        self.strength_score = strength_score
66	        self.source_domain = source_domain
67	        self.target_domain = target_domain
68	        self.source_framework = source_framework
69	        self.target_framework = target_framework
70	
71	
72	class ControlMetadata:
73	    """Metadata for a single SCF control node."""
74	    __slots__ = (
75	        "control_id", "domain", "framework",
76	        "maturity_level", "mcr_dsr",
77	    )
78	
79	    def __init__(
80	        self,
81	        control_id: str,
82	        domain: str,
83	        framework: str,
84	        maturity_level: str | None = None,
85	        mcr_dsr: Literal["MCR", "DSR", None] = None,
86	    ) -> None:
87	        self.control_id = control_id
88	        self.domain = domain
89	        self.framework = framework
90	        self.maturity_level = maturity_level
91	        self.mcr_dsr = mcr_dsr
92	
93	
94	# ── Phase guard ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
95	
96	class SAPViolationError(Exception):
97	    """Raised when an agent attempts to access test data before Gate 2."""
98	    pass
99	
100	
101	def assert_gate2_approved(state: HCGRCState) -> None:
102	    """
103	    Raise SAPViolationError if Gate 2 has not been approved.
104	
105	    Must be called before any agent accesses test_ids or test split data.
106	    This is the primary enforcement mechanism for the pre-registration firewall.
107	    """
108	    gate_status = state.get("gate_status", {})
109	    gate_2 = gate_status.get("gate_2")
110	    if gate_2 is None or gate_2.get("decision") != "approved":
111	        raise SAPViolationError(
112	            "Attempted to access test split before Gate 2 approval. "
113	            "This is a SAP violation. Gate 2 status: "
114	            f"{gate_2.get('decision') if gate_2 else 'not_run'}. "
115	            "Do not access test_ids or confirmatory split until gate_2.decision == 'approved'."
116	        )
117	
118	
119	# ── Base agent ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
120	
121	class BaseResearchAgent(ABC):
122	    """
123	    Abstract base for all HC-GRC Phase 1 research agents.
124	
125	    Subclasses implement run_exploratory() and run_confirmatory() separately.
126	    The __call__ method dispatches based on state["phase"].
127	
128	    Protected agents set PROTECTED = True — checked by Agent Evolution before
129	    any prompt modification.
130	    """
131	
132	    PROTECTED: bool = False  # override in P1–P5, statistical-analyst, hypothesis-formalizer
133	    AGENT_ID: str = ""       # kebab-case, matches AGENT.md name field
134	    # Set True once the agent's run_* methods are real. While False, a
135	    # NotImplementedError is an expected Phase-0/1 stub; once True, a
136	    # NotImplementedError is a genuine bug the orchestrator must not silently
137	    # swallow as 'stub_pending' (#202).
138	    IMPLEMENTED: bool = False
139	
140	    def __call__(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
141	        """
142	        LangGraph-compatible call: returns a partial state update dict.
143	
144	        Dispatches to run_exploratory() or run_confirmatory() based on phase.
145	
146	        The canonical phase values written by the graph are phase_0 / phase_1 /
147	        phase_2 (see state.Phase). Exploratory analysis runs in phase_0/phase_1;
148	        confirmatory analysis runs in phase_2, where the Gate 2 SAP firewall
149	        (assert_gate2_approved) must hold before any test-split access. The legacy
150	        string aliases "exploratory"/"confirmatory" are accepted defensively.
151	        (Hardening pass 1, #17: phase_2 previously hit the else-branch and raised,
152	        so run_confirmatory and the SAP firewall were unreachable.)
153	        """
154	        phase = state.get("phase", "phase_0")
155	        if phase in ("phase_0", "phase_1", "exploratory"):
156	            return self.run_exploratory(state)
157	        elif phase in ("phase_2", "confirmatory"):
158	            assert_gate2_approved(state)
159	            return self.run_confirmatory(state)
160	        else:
161	            raise ValueError(f"Unknown phase: {phase!r}")
162	
163	    @abstractmethod
164	    def run_exploratory(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
165	        """
166	        Execute exploratory analysis on train + val splits.
167	
168	        All output artifact filenames must be prefixed EXP_.
169	        No p-value decision language in any exploratory output.
170	        Must not touch test_ids.
171	        """
172	        ...
173	
174	    @abstractmethod
175	    def run_confirmatory(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
176	        """
177	        Execute confirmatory analysis on test split for one hypothesis.
178	
179	        Gate 2 approval is verified by __call__ before this is invoked.
180	        Every confirmatory script must carry a SAP header:
181	          # SAP header: hypothesis_id=H[x].[n], test=[named test],
182	          #             effect_size_threshold=[value], decision_rule=[criterion]
183	        """
184	        ...
185	
186	
187	# ── Base pipeline agent ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
188	
189	class BasePipelineAgent(ABC):
190	    """
191	    Abstract base for data pipeline agents (acquisition, curation, steward, embedding).
192	
193	    Pipeline agents prepare and version the research corpus. They do not perform
194	    exploratory or confirmatory analysis — they have no run_exploratory() /
195	    run_confirmatory() distinction. They run once at project initialization and
196	    re-run when the SCF corpus is updated.
197	
198	    Pipeline agents are never PROTECTED — they contain no analysis logic that
199	    could affect research conclusions. Agent Evolution may modify them without
200	    Escalation approval.
201	
202	    The run() return dict is a partial HCGRCState update, merged by the graph runtime.
203	    Agents must never mutate state in place.
204	    """
205	
206	    AGENT_ID: str = ""     # kebab-case, matches AGENT.md name field
207	    PROTECTED: bool = False  # never True for pipeline agents
208	    IMPLEMENTED: bool = False  # see BaseResearchAgent.IMPLEMENTED (#202)
209	
210	    @abstractmethod
211	    def run(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
212	        """
213	        Execute the pipeline stage and return a partial HCGRCState update.
214	
215	        Raises NotImplementedError until the real SCF data pipeline is live
216	        on the Mac mini with DVC and Qdrant configured.
217	        """
218	        ...
219

101	    def _tag_nist_cluster(self, node_attrs: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:
102	        """
103	        Add nist_cluster=True to node attributes for NIST framework nodes.
104	
105	        Must be applied during graph construction so community detection
106	        reports can flag NIST-internal communities.
107	        """

109	        node_attrs["nist_cluster"] = framework.upper() in {
110	            "NIST 800-53", "NIST CSF", "NIST 800-171"
111	        }

1	"""
2	P2 Agent — Control Space Topology.
3	
4	PURPOSE:
5	Constructs a directed graph from STRM mappings (controls as nodes, mappings
6	as edges) and characterizes the network's mathematical properties. Identifies
7	hub controls, bridge controls, isolated domains, and community structure.
8	
9	PROTECTED AGENT: Prompts may not be modified autonomously by Agent Evolution.
10	Any modification requires Escalation approval (ADR-0015 §77).
11	
12	WORKFLOW POSITION:
13	  Embedding Agent → P2 → P3 (community assignments), P4 (hub map),
14	                          Statistical Analyst, Report Agent
15	
16	EDGE DIRECTIONALITY:
17	  subset (⊂) and superset (⊃): directed
18	  equivalence (=) and intersection (∩): undirected (add edges in both directions)
19	  strength_score: edge weight; imputed at median if missing (document in codebook)
20	
21	COMMUNITY DETECTION:
22	  Must run at least Leiden AND Louvain — results reported for both.
23	  Single-algorithm community structure is not reported as authoritative.
24	
25	NIST CLUSTER CONSTRAINT:
26	  NIST 800-53, CSF, 800-171 node attributes tagged as one authorship cluster.
27	  Community membership spanning all three is NOT evidence of independent convergence.
28	
29	See AGENT.md: agents/03-analysis/p2-control-topology/AGENT.md

content.text:
Now let me review my findings. I have all the key information I need. Let me create a comprehensive analysis:

## Analysis Summary

Based on careful reading of the actual code and the cited specifications:

**CODE INSPECTION:**
- File: `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc/src/agents/p2_control_topology/agent.py:109-110`
- The `_tag_nist_cluster` method checks: `framework.upper() in {"NIST 800-53", "NIST CSF", "NIST 800-171"}`
- The method uses **space-separated** framework identifiers

**SPECIFICATION INSPECTION:**
- FRAMEWORK_RELATIONSHIPS.md, Table in Section 1 (lines 11-17):
  - NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 → **Code: `NIST-800-53`** (kebab-case)
  - NIST CSF 2.0 → **Code: `NIST-CSF`** (kebab-case)
  - NIST SP 800-171 Rev 3 → **Code: `NIST-800-171`** (kebab-case)

- AGENT.md, Line 77:
  - States: "NIST 800-53, CSF, 800-171 node attributes are tagged as one authorship cluster"
  - This is the **specification requirement** — using space-separated names in text

**CRITICAL FINDING:**

The candidate claims a mismatch, but the spec actually supports **both** representations:

1. **FRAMEWORK_RELATIONSHIPS.md** defines the *canonical framework codes* (kebab-case) used for data interchange and system identifiers: `NIST-800-53`, `NIST-CSF`, `NIST-800-171`

2. **AGENT.md and the code docstring** use the *human-readable names* (space-separated) for specification and documentation: "NIST 800-53, CSF, 800-171"

3. **The actual code in `_tag_nist_cluster`** checks for space-separated values, which aligns with the **human-facing specification** in AGENT.md line 77 and the code's own docstring (line 26).

**VERDICT:**

The code is checking for the **documented specification requirement** (spaces), not the kebab-cased data identifiers. However, **the real defect** is that the code never actually documents:

1. Whether the source `node_attrs["framework"]` field contains space-separated or kebab-cased values
2. Where `node_attrs` comes from (the ARA artifacts metadata per AGENT.md)
3. Any normalization or format transformation between the canonical codes and the node attribute values

**This is a MINOR RESEARCH INTEGRITY ISSUE** — not critical:
- The code is NOT implemented (both `run_exploratory` and `run_confirmatory` raise `NotImplementedError`)
- There is no data flowing through it yet
- This is a Phase-0 stub, so the bug won't manifest until Phase 1 implementation

But IF the framework field contains kebab-case values (per the canonical codes), the check will fail silently.

input.title: NIST framework identifier mismatch in _tag_nist_cluster — Phase-0 stub, real defect if data contains kebab-case
input.verification: Code inspection of /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc/src/agents/p2_control_topology/agent.py lines 101-112 confirms the _tag_nist_cluster method checks for space-separated identifiers ("NIST 800-53", "NIST CSF", "NIST 800-171"). FRAMEWORK_RELATIONSHIPS.md section 1 table (lines 11-17) defines canonical codes as kebab-case (NIST-800-53, NIST-CSF, NIST-800-171). AGENT.md line 77 specifies the human-facing constraint using spaces. The specification does not document: (1) the actual format of the framework field in node_attrs, (2) where node_attrs["framework"] is populated, (3) whether normalization occurs. Both run_exploratory() and run_confirmatory() raise NotImplementedError, confirming this is a Phase-0 stub. No actual data flows through this code yet. The defect is REAL (format mismatch will cause silent failures) but LATENT (blocked by stub implementation).
input.recommended_action: In Phase 1 implementation: (1) Document the source format of framework values in node_attrs (audit ARA artifacts metadata to confirm kebab-case vs space-separated). (2) If framework values are kebab-case per canonical codes, update _tag_nist_cluster to check for {"NIST-800-53", "NIST-CSF", "NIST-800-171"} OR add normalization that converts kebab-case to spaces before checking. (3) Add a test case that verifies _tag_nist_cluster correctly identifies NIST frameworks regardless of input format. (4) Document the expected format in the method docstring.
